content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

March 15, 1991


Lee La Fave
Field Representative for
Assemblymember Robert C. Frazee
Member of the Assembly
Seventy-Fourth District
3088 Pio Pico Drive, Suite 200
Carlsbad, CA 92008

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-91-135

Dear Mr. La Fave:

You have requested confirmation of telephone advice provided
to you concerning the new gift and honoraria limits of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

This letter confirms that your letter dated March 1, 1991,
accurately summarizes the telephone advice I provided to you on
that date. As we discussed in our telephone conversation, the Act
now provides a gift limit for designated employees of the
legislature.

Section 89505(a) provides, in pertinent part:

No member of a state board or commission, and
no designated employee of a state agency, shall
accept gifts with a total value of more than two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) in a calendar year
from any single source, if the member or employee
would be required to report the receipt of income
or gifts from that source on his or her statement
of economic interest.

In our telephone conversation of March 1, 1991 you asked
whether a payment for travel and accommodations paid by the Orange
County Water District in connection with a tour of Northern
California Water Projects including the Oroville Dam was either a
gift or honorarium under the Act. You were not invited to speak,

_______________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

File No. A-91-135
Page 2

but only to view the facilities. You stated as the Field
Representative for Assemblymember Frazee, you believed you were
subject to full disclosure under the Act.

I explained that Section 89504(b)(1) provides that where a
public official receives a gift of travel, Section 89506 controls
whether the gift is subject to the gift limit. Section 89506
provides that a gift of travel is subject to the limits of the
Act, except that:

(a) Payments, advances, or reimbursements,
for travel, including actual transportation and
related lodging and subsistence which is reasonably
related to a legislative or governmental purpose,
or to an issue of state, national, or international
public policy, is not prohibited or limited by this
chapter if either of the following apply:

(1) The travel is in connection with a speech
given by the elected state officer, member of a
state board or commission, or designated employee
of a state agency, the lodging and subsistence
expenses are limited to the day immediately
preceding, the day of, and the day immediately
following the speech, and the travel is within the
United States.

(2) The travel is provided by a government, a
governmental agency, a foreign government, a
governmental authority, a bona fide public or
private educational institution, as defined in
Section 203 of the Revenue and Taxation Code, or a
nonprofit charitable or religious organization
which is exempt from taxation under Section
501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code, or by a
person domiciled outside the United States which
substantially satisfies the requirements for tax
exempt status under Section 501(c)(3) of the
Internal Revenue Code.

Section 89506(a)(1)-(2), emphasis added.

Your travel and accommodations are being paid by the Orange
County Water District, a government agency. A tour of state
water projects as Assemblymember Frazee's representative appears
reasonably related to a legislative or governmental purpose or to
issues of state public policy. This is particularly so if, as you
have indicated, the Assemblymember has a long-term interest in
water issues and legislation. We would conclude that pursuant to
Section 89506, the payments are not subject to the gift limits of
the Act.

However, as I explained in our telephone conversation, absent
an express exception to the definition of "gift" in Section 82028,
the receipt of a gift of free travel and accommodations would be

File No. A-91-135
Page 3


disclosable on your annual statement of economic interests.2  As
we discussed in our telephone conversation, while the costs
incurred by the county for the tour itself would be "informational
materials" and exempt under the Act, the exception does not exempt
travel to and from the location of the tour except under very
narrow circumstances.  (Sect. 82028(b)(1); In re Spellman (1975)
1 FPPC Ops. 16; Leidigh Advice Letter, No. A-89-248, copies
enclosed.)

     Also, please be aware that Section 87100, et seq., of the Act
requires that public officials who have received gifts of $250 or
more from a single source must disqualify themselves from all
governmental decisions that will foreseeably and materially affect
the donor.   If a decision should come before you in the future
concerning the Orange County Water District, you should contact us
for advice about potential conflicts of interest.  In addition,
please note that the Commission will be considering regulations
concerning travel under the new gift limits in the near future.

     I trust this letter has addressed your concerns.  Should you
have any further questions regarding this matter please feel free
to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        By: John W. Wallace
                                            Counsel, Legal Division
SH:JWW:plh

Enclosures




2    Intrastate travel in connection with making a speech or with
substantive participation in a panel or seminar is currently
exempt from disclosure pursuant to Regulation 18728 (copy
enclosed).  However, the Commission is expected to review this
regulation in the near future.

sections.question: Whether a payment for travel and accommodations paid by the Orange County Water District in connection with a tour of Northern California Water Projects including the Oroville Dam was either a gift or honorarium under the Act, and whether as the Field Representative for Assemblymember Frazee, the recipient was subject to full disclosure under the Act.
sections.conclusion: Pursuant to Section 89506, the payments are not subject to the gift limits of the Act because the travel is provided by a governmental agency and reasonably relates to a legislative or governmental purpose. However, absent an express exception to the definition of 'gift' in Section 82028, the receipt of free travel and accommodations would be disclosable on the annual statement of economic interests.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether travel and accommodations provided by a governmental agency (Orange County Water District) for a legislative representative to tour state water projects constitutes a reportable gift or honorarium under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The travel and accommodations are exempt from gift limits under Section 89506(a)(2) because they are provided by a governmental agency for a purpose reasonably related to legislative/governmental matters, but must still be disclosed on statements of economic interest unless an exception applies.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is FPPC response letter confirming prior telephone advice. Contains verbatim statutory citations and legal analysis. Confidence slightly reduced due to OCR-like formatting issues in source document, but core content is clearly extractable.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a payment for travel and accommodations paid by the Orange County Water District in connection with a tour of Northern California Water Projects including the Oroville Dam was either a gift or honorarium under the Act, and whether as the Field Representative for Assemblymember Frazee, the recipient was subject to full disclosure under the Act.

CONCLUSION: Pursuant to Section 89506, the payments are not subject to the gift limits of the Act because the travel is provided by a governmental agency and reasonably relates to a legislative or governmental purpose. However, absent an express exception to the definition of 'gift' in Section 82028, the receipt of free travel and accommodations would be disclosable on the annual statement of economic interests.

embedding.summary: FPPC confirms telephone advice regarding gift and honoraria limits under the Political Reform Act. The letter advises that travel and accommodations paid by Orange County Water District for a legislative representative to tour water projects are not subject to gift limits but remain disclosable on economic interest statements.
